query: Generative complaints OR frustrating OR broken

results.title: The Most Frustrating Aspects of Generative AI | Rightpoint
results.content:
Don’t get us wrong. We’re bullish on Generative AI and the positive impacts it can have and will continue to have on reshaping industries and organizations, but, as with any new innovations, it can also be a source of frustration for many. We’re going to share some of the frustrations we often hear from our clients and our point of view on how to deal with them.

## Why Are There So Many Tools That Do the Exact Same Thing? [...] # The Most Frustrating Aspects of Generative AI

Copy of 214A9883
Copy of 214A9883

Here at Rightpoint, we are believers in the transformational power of AI. Generative AI is a fascinating paradigm shift for customer, employee, and product experiences. We've seen many of our customers begin this shift, whether enabling their employees to be more efficient with Generative AI tools or reimagining customer experiences with Generative AI human-like interactions.

While this is truly a once-in-a-generation change in how we all work and serve our various customers, it is important to recognize how wild the last 18+ months have been. Almost every client conversation, statement of work, or project deliverable these days has the question, “Well, what about AI?” [...] In this case, extreme competition is breeding extreme confusion. There are some platforms that are creating holistic, end-to-end Generative AI solutions for your workforce, while others are focused on niche industry, process, or moment-specific use cases. And right now, the market is messy with a lot of competing platforms – some early winners, some emerging players, and others fading. We are stuck in cycles of change that will make your head spin if you follow too closely.

results.title: Generative AI Customer Service: Automate Customer Complaints | SS&C Blue Prism
results.content:
The complaint email is picked up by our business process management (BPM) solution, SS&C | Blue Prism® Chorus. Chorus is going to act as the orchestrator in this process while a digital worker (also called an AI worker) utilizes generative AI to craft a response.

Generative AI Customer Service Complaints

In the first step, generative AI will help personalize an initial response. Instead of using a generic email template or having your customer support agent type out a full response, gen AI uses its natural language processes (NLP) capabilities to understand the context of Rebecca’s original complaint.

Generative AI Customer Service Complaint [...] ## 

### Example 2 - Generative AI Customer Service

In this second example, Helen is unhappy with the customer service she has received. She lodges a complaint via email.

In this case, the digital worker locates the reference to poor customer service and the representative involved, and gen AI composes an email containing the relevant data. It types up a response, assuring Helen that her complaint will be examined and addressed.

Generative AI Customer Service [...] Generative AI Customer Service Complaint

Using that knowledge base, the gen AI agent is going to write out a response a lot quicker than a person could and flag any issues as it works. In this example, Rebecca didn’t provide her policy number, so the digital worker asked for more details. The policy number is needed so the insurer can understand the reason for Rebecca’s premium increase.

At this point, gen AI marks the complaint as complete since it cannot be fulfilled with the information given. It has extracted the information we do know and categorized the complaint type, which can now be used for reporting purposes.

Generative AI Customer Service Complaint

Watch demo video below to see how you can use generative AI in customer service

results.title: AI Customer Feedback Analysis with Generative AI: Turn Voice‑of‑Customer into Action
results.content:
2. The Ripple Effect of Unresolved Complaints

Negative feedback left unaddressed can have far-reaching consequences. One disgruntled customer can quickly multiply into many, as word-of-mouth, amplified by social media, can tarnish your business’s reputation. This damage can be challenging to repair.

3. Limited Effectiveness of Rule-based Analysis [...] 1. Slow Response to Negative Feedback: A Detriment to Your Business

When customers express dissatisfaction, time is of the essence. A prompt response can transform a negative situation into a positive one, demonstrating your value for customers and commitment to resolution. However, traditional feedback systems often move at a glacial pace, involving multiple steps, including collection, review, and action planning. This delay can leave customers feeling neglected, potentially driving them to seek alternatives.

2. The Ripple Effect of Unresolved Complaints [...] Collect feedback from multiple channels (surveys, reviews, chat logs, social media).
 Identify key themes by grouping similar comments and topics.
 Summarize insights clearly using advanced language models.
 Highlight sentiment and urgency to pinpoint important issues.
 Trigger actions and alerts so teams can quickly respond.

By streamlining these steps, generative AI transforms the way businesses manage customer feedback. Instead of struggling with slow processes and overwhelming data, companies can now proactively address common feedback challenges and unlock significant benefits, including:

1. Instant Response to Negative Feedback:

results.title: Lessons Leaned: I Built a Successful Generative AI Chatbot Failure
results.content:
This can leave Foreign Service staff in the State Department, USAID, and elsewhere lost. They then overwhelm EXOs with basic questions, errors can happen even with good intentions, and money can be wasted on trying to comply. Worse, rules can be broken and careers damaged through plain ignorance and confusion.

## My LLM Inspiration

Large language models (LLMs) that power Generative AI solutions intuitively understand large bodies of text. They are skilled in presenting dense government rules or verbose corporate regulations in easily understood human-readable forms. This is what GenAI does best. [...] Now it didn’t have the full functionally of my original policy bot. It didn’t give a relevance score for each link, it was wonky in some answers, and there wasn’t a clear way for users to give training feedback. Still, it was usable, and I received very positive feedback.

## Chatbot Market Failure

The technology challenges were annoying but not a real problem. We could correct and adapt as we improved the software and user interface. Regardless, users loved the chatbot experience.

I received dozens of emails shortly after launch praising the FAM Policy Chatbot. It was helping staff subject to the FAM, to understand its rules. It was relieving pressure on EXO to answer routine queries. It showed that creating a tool to parse the FAM was possible with easily-accessible tools.

results.title: How we use generative AI to transform customer complaints into opportunities | NatWest Group posted on the topic | LinkedIn
results.content:
We believe that every complaint is not just a challenge, but an opportunity to build trust and confidence with our customers. Led by Tammy Martin, our Head of Customer Experience and Contact, we’re transforming how we handle complaints through the innovative use of generative AI. This isn't just about technology; it’s about a cultural shift. By involving our colleagues in the design process, we’ve fostered an environment of trust and collaboration. The result? Colleagues who are not just adapting to AI, but championing it. Our AI-powered solutions not only generate accurate and empathetic responses in minutes but also empower our colleagues to focus on what truly matters: building meaningful connections with our customers. Kyle Danko 

Client Account Leadership Senior Manager [...] + Report this post

  I’ve been fortunate enough to attend Data & AI talks & meet-ups lately and one concept stuck: Agentic AI Design Patterns. One team shared three: Workflow, Hybrid, Orchestration. All useful and simple (I like it) — but then what about state? Feels less like another pattern, more like the foundation depending on the underlying systems it might be interacting with. Without it, workflows and orchestration might easily break, won’t reconcile and lose relevancy. The challenge: local vs global state, persistence for long processes and Model Context Protocols MCP pushing new ways to think about abstraction. Maybe the real challenge isn’t which pattern — but how we design for state so the patterns remain simple, modular and hold together at scale.
